paper_title: DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the R Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large Language Model (LLM) agents can automate data-science workflows, but many rigorous statistical methods implemented in R remain underused because LLMs struggle with statistical knowledge and tool retrieval. Existing retrieval-augmented approaches focus on function-level semantics and ignore data distribution, producing suboptimal matches. We propose DARE (Distribution-Aware Retrieval Embedding), a lightweight, plug-and-play retrieval model that incorporates data distribution information into function representations for R package retrieval. Our main contributions are: (i) RPKB, a curated R Package Knowledge Base derived from 8,191 high-quality CRAN packages; (ii) DARE, an embedding model that fuses distributional features with function metadata to improve retrieval relevance; and (iii) RCodingAgent, an R-oriented LLM agent for reliable R code generation and a suite of statistical analysis tasks for systematically evaluating LLM agents in realistic analytical scenarios. Empirically, DARE achieves an NDCG at 10 of 93.47%, outperforming state-of-the-art open-source embedding models by up to 17% on package retrieval while using substantially fewer parameters. Integrating DARE into RCodingAgent yields significant gains on downstream analysis tasks. This work helps narrow the gap between LLM automation and the mature R statistical ecosystem.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval Maojun Sun * 1 Yue Wu * 1 Yifei Xie * 1 Ruijian Han 1 Binyan Jiang 1 Defeng Sun 2 Yancheng Yuan 2 Jian Huang 1 2 (cid:128) Project Website DARE-R-Retriever

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large Language Model (LLM) agents can automate data-science workflows, but many rigorous statistical methods implemented in remain underused because LLMs struggle with statistical knowledge and tool retrieval. Existing retrieval-augmented approaches focus on function-level semantics and ignore data distribution, producing suboptimal matches. We propose DARE (Distribution-Aware Retrieval Embedding), lightweight, plug-and-play retrieval model that incorporates data distribution information into function representations for package retrieval. Our main contributions are: (i) RPKB, curated Package Knowledge Base derived from 8,191 high-quality CRAN packages; (ii) DARE, an embedding model that fuses distributional features with function metadata to improve retrieval relevance; and (iii) RCodingAgent, an R-oriented LLM agent for reliable code generation and suite of statistical analysis tasks for systematically evaluating LLM agents in realistic analytical scenarios. Empirically, DARE achieves an NDCG@10 of 93.47%, outperforming state-of-the-art open-source embedding models by up to 17% on package retrieval while using substantially fewer parameters. Integrating DARE into RCodingAgent yields significant gains on downstream analysis tasks. This work helps narrow the gap between LLM automation and the mature statistical ecosystem. 1Department of Data Science and Artificial Intelligence, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hong Kong SAR, China 2Department of Applied Mathematics, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hong Kong SAR, China. Correspondence to: Ruijian Han <ruijian.han@polyu.edu.hk>, Yancheng Yuan <yancheng.yuan@polyu.edu.hk>, Jian Huang <j.huang@polyu.edu.hk>. Preprint. March 6, 2026. 1 Figure 1. Comparison of traditional semantic function search methods and Distribution-Aware Retrieval Embedding (DARE) method. 1. Introduction Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents have rapidly emerged as promising paradigm for automating data science workflows (Chen et al., 2025; Sun et al., 2025b). By integrating natural language reasoning, code generation, and tool execution, these agents are increasingly capable of translating user intent expressed in natural language into executable analytical pipelines. Recent systems have demonstrated strong performance in tasks such as data preprocessing, exploratory analysis, feature engineering, and model training (Sun et al., 2026), significantly reducing the manual effort traditionally required in end-to-end data science processes (Zhang et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2025a; Hong et al., 2025; Zhang et al., 2025). Despite these advancements, existing LLM-based data science agents predominantly operate within Python-centric ecosystems (Orlanski et al., 2023; Zhao & Fard, 2025), and have limited capability in utilizing R, language and environment specifically designed for statistical computing and analysis (R Core Team, 2025). This limitation results in the underutilization of decades of accumulated statistical knowledge. The statistical community has long developed rich repertoire of theoretically rigorous methodologies for data analysis, many of which are implemented and actively maintained within the ecosystem (Ihaka & Gentleman, 1996; Core Team, 2025). Central to DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval this ecosystem is the Comprehensive Archive Network (CRAN, https://cran.r-project.org/), which hosts thousands of peer-reviewed, highly specialized packages that encode domain expertise and methodological best practices. However, current LLMs are trained on corpus that are heavily skewed toward general-purpose programming languages, especially Python. As result, LLM-based agents often exhibit systematic performance gaps when interacting with R-based programming (Orlanski et al., 2023; Zhao & Fard, 2025). In practice, this imbalance leads to two major issues: (i) agents frequently default to Python-based implementations even when provides more statistically appropriate or computationally efficient solutions, and (ii) when attempting to generate code, agents often hallucinate function names, misuse parameter configurations, or fail to identify the correct statistical package. natural strategy to mitigate these limitations is RAG (Lewis et al., 2020), where agents retrieve external documentation or function descriptions from package repositories. Existing embedding models primarily rely on semantic similarity between user queries and textual descriptions of functions. However, the applicability of statistical methods often depends not only on semantic intent but also on data distribution characteristics, such as sparsity structure, dimensionality regimes, distributional assumptions, and modality-specific constraints. General-purpose embedding models, trained on broad web corpora, frequently fail to capture these subtle yet crucial distributional conditions, resulting in retrieval errors that propagate to downstream code generation and execution failures. To address this limitation, we first construct RPKB (R Package Knowledge Base), curated dataset derived from 8,191 high-quality packages on CRAN (https:// cran.r-project.org/), with functions spanning diverse statistical domains. RPKB provides structured function metadata, documentation, and usage information, serving as valuable resource for statistical tool retrieval and LLM tool learning. Example entries and the data schema in RPKB are given in Section of the Supplementary Materials. Building upon RPKB, we propose DARE (DistributionAware Retrieval Embedding), retrieval model that explicitly incorporates data distribution into function representations for retrieving packages. By training contrastive dual-encoder architecture conditioned on data profiles, DARE learns to distinguish between functions that are semantically similar but statistically incompatible under different data contexts. Importantly, DARE is designed as lightweight and plug-and-play retrieval module that can be seamlessly integrated into LLM-based agent systems like LAMBDA (Sun et al., 2025a). Experimental results demonstrate that DARE achieves state-of-the-art retrieval performance on RPKB, achieving an NDCG@10 of 93.47% (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain, high-quality information retrieval ranking metric), outperforming prior state-of-the-art embedding models by up to 17% while using only 23M parameters. Figure 1 illustrates the difference between traditional semantic retrieval and DARE. To investigate the practical impact of integrating DARE into LLM agents for real-world statistical analysis workflows, we further design RCodingAgent, an end-to-end Roriented LLM agent that supports automated statistical analysis through iterative reasoning, statistical tool retrieval, code generation, and execution-based validation. To systematically evaluate agent performance, we construct diverse suite of 16 carefully designed R-based statistical analysis tasks spanning representative statistical domains, including hypothesis testing, goodness-of-fit analysis, survival analysis, and mixed-effects modeling. Experimental results show that integrating DARE into RCodingAgent significantly improves downstream statistical analysis performance by up to 56.25% across both frontier and lightweight LLMs, demonstrating its effectiveness in enabling reliable automated statistical workflows. Our key contributions are summarized as follows: 1. We construct RPKB, curated repository derived from 8,191 high-quality packages and functions spanning diverse statistical domains. RPKB provides structured statistical tool knowledge and serves as valuable resource for LLM-based tool learning and retrieval. 2. We propose DARE, lightweight and plug-and-play distribution-aware retrieval embedding model that incorporates data distribution constraints into function representations. DARE achieves state-of-the-art retrieval performance on RPKB, outperforming prior embedding models with substantially larger parameter counts, while maintaining high computational efficiency. 3. We design RCodingAgent, an end-to-end R-oriented LLM agent that automates statistical analysis workflows, and construct diverse suite of 16 R-based statistical analysis tasks for evaluation. When integrated into RCodingAgent, DARE significantly improves downstream statistical analysis performance. 2. Related Works LLM Agents for Data Science. Recent works have empowered LLMs to act as data science agents by equipping them with planing, code execution environments, selfcorrection, and report generation (Sun et al., 2025b; Hong 2 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval Figure 2. The overall framework of DARE training process. et al., 2025). While these agents show promise, they predominantly rely on Python. Efforts to incorporate are limited and often suffer from lower code generation quality due to the scarcity of in training data relative to Python (Li et al., 2023). Dense Retrieval and RAG. Dense retrieval, which maps queries and documents into shared latent space via biencoders, has evolved significantly from pioneering architectures like DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) and Contriever (Izacard et al., 2021). Recent advancements have shifted towards scaling up parameter counts and leveraging large-scale instruction tuning. Advanced models such as Snowflake Arctic (Merrick, 2024), Gte-large-en-v1.5 (Zhang et al., 2024), and Mxbai (Lee et al., 2024) have achieved dominance on general benchmarks by utilizing massive pre-training corpora and complex multi-stage fine-tuning strategies. However, these models are unable to fully utilize their abilities in statistical computing scenarios. First, their general-purpose design primarily emphasizes semantic similarity, making it difficult to capture statistical compatibility factors beyond textual semantics, such as data distribution characteristics and modeling assumptions. Second, the substantial increase in model size introduces significant computational overhead during retrieval. This limitation becomes particularly pronounced when searching over large-scale statistical tool repositories, such as extensive package databases, where low-latency retrieval is critical for interactive agent workflows. To address these limitations, we introduce the DARE model, which achieves strong retrieval accuracy and efficiency by explicitly incorporating data distribution information into the representation learning process, enabling distribution-aware statistical tool retrieval. Tool Learning. Tool learning aims to teach LLMs to use external APIs (Wang et al., 2022; Shen et al., 2023). Most existing approaches rely on in-context learning (ICL) with API descriptions, which tightly couples tool selection with generation. Our work differs by introducing dedicated, data-aware retrieval module that decouples tool selection from generation, enabling scalable access to thousands of statistical functions. When augmented with DARE, LLM agents are able to effectively utilize broader range of statistical packages and achieve substantial performance improvements in downstream data analysis tasks. 3. Methodology In this section, we present details of the database construction, proposed DARE framework (Figure 2), RCodingAgent and the evaluation methods. 3.1. Database Construction We constructed specialized knowledge base by curating packages from the Comprehensive Archive Network (CRAN)1. Our data pipeline consists of three stages: 1https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/ available_packages_by_name.html DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval 1. Extraction: We crawled raw documentation (HTML and PDF) from CRAN, extracting package-level metadata (e.g., Package Name, Description, Version, Author) and granular function-level details, specifically the Description, Usage, Arguments, and Value sections. 2. Function-Level Chunking and Filtering: We processed the corpus at the function level. To ensure the density of statistical knowledge, we implemented rigorous filtering pipeline. We excluded: (i) generic utility functions (e.g., I/O operations, basic string manipulation); (ii) functions with vague or incomplete descriptions; (iii) auxiliary methods lacking specific algorithmic content. Our selection focused strictly on core statistical primitives and computational algorithms with clear analytical objectives. 3. Data Profile Generation: To achieve modeling on the data context, we employed Grok-4.1-fast to synthesize metadata from the unstructured documentation. The LLM was prompted to infer key statistical data attributes, such as data modality, distribution assumptions, and dimensionality. 4. Storage: The curated data were indexed using ChromaDB. The final repository comprises 8,191 highquality functions spanning diverse statistical domains, serving as the retrieval corpora F. More details about the database construction and selection, some examples and generation prompts are provided in the Supplementary Materials. 3.2. Problem Formulation Let = {fk}F k=1 denote repository of candidate functions. Each function is formally defined as tuple = (d, cd), where denotes the natural-language documentation (e.g., package and function description), and cd denotes the structured data profile encoding the functions inherent constraints (e.g., data modality, distribution, and dimensionality). In the inference stage, the user provides natural-language request together with dataset c. Based on this information, the system derives query-side data profile cq, which can be automatically inferred from dataset characteristics and signals extracted from the user query (e.g., data modality: tabular, feature type: numerical, distribution assumption: non-gaussian, dimensionality: high). Our goal is to retrieve the most appropriate function ˆf that satisfies both the semantic intent of and the datalevel context of cq: ˆf (q, cq) = arg max s(cid:0)(q, cq), (cid:1). 3.3. DARE Modeling We utilize bi-encoder architecture with shared weights initialized from sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6v2 (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019). We define the shared encoder network as ε(), which maps input texts into shared m-dimensional vector space. Let [ ; ] denote the textual concatenation operation. The agents request is encoded into query embedding eq = ε([q ; cq]) Rm. Correspondingly, each candidate function is encoded into function embedding ef = ε([d ; cd]) Rm. We compute the relevance score using cosine similarity between these representations: s(cid:0)eq, ef (cid:1) = cos(eq, ef ) = ef eq2 ef 2 . This factorization allows for efficient retrieval via Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS) over the precomputed function embeddings {ef }f . We construct supervised retrieval dataset = {(qi, cq,i, fi)}M i=1, where fi denotes the ground-truth function (comprising documentation di and profile cd,i) for the i-th query-context pair. We finetune the encoder using the InfoNCE (van den Oord et al., 2019) objective with in-batch negatives. Given mini-batch of size , for each anchor query i, we treat the paired function fi as the positive sample and all other functions fj (j = i) in the batch as negatives. The loss function for the i-th sample is defined as: Li = log exp(cid:0)cos(eqi , efi)/τ (cid:1) exp(cid:0)cos(eqi, efj )/τ (cid:1) (cid:80) j=1 , (cid:80)N where τ is learnable temperature parameter. The total batch loss is computed as = 1 i=1 Li. This objective maximizes the similarity between the query embedding eqi and its corresponding function embedding efi, while simultaneously minimizing the similarity to other candidates efj that do not align with the querys semantic and distributional constraints. Figure 2 illustrates the framework of DARE. 3.4. RCodingAgent: DARE-Augmented Agentic Data

sections.title: Analysis
sections.content: To support agent-based statistical analysis using the programming language, we design and implement RCodingAgent, prototype LLM-based agent that performs iterative reasoning, tool retrieval, code generation, and executionbased validation to complete end-to-end analytical tasks. As illustrated in Figure 3, RCodingAgent incorporates the DARE module to enhance downstream 4 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval Figure 3. An example of RCodingAgent for realistic statistical analysis. statistical analysis workflows. Specifically, given naturallanguage query q, the agent first invokes DARE to retrieve candidate packages and functions that satisfy both analytical intent and data compatibility constraints. The retrieved functions are returned with structured metadata, including argument specifications and usage examples, which are injected into the LLM context to guide tool invocation and code generation via in-context learning, enabling accurate and executable statistical analysis. 3.5. Evaluating LLM Agents for Statistical

sections.title: Programming in R
sections.content: To systematically evaluate LLM agents in realistic, execution-grounded statistical programming workflows, we design an evaluation framework based on suite of 16 representative R-based statistical analysis tasks. The evaluation focuses on assessing whether agents can correctly generate executable code, and produce statistically valid analytical outputs under realistic data contexts. Upper panel of Figure 4 illustrates the task construction pipeline, bottom panel shows an overview of selected domains and packages. Specifically, we collect high-quality example scripts from the RPKB repository covering diverse statistical and scientific domains, including hypothesis testing, goodnessof-fit analysis, survival analysis, graphical model estimation, mixed-effects modeling, and statistical preprocessing. These scripts are executed to produce verified ground-truth outputs. Based on the underlying datasets and analytical 5 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval Figure 4. Upper panel: Pipeline for constructing R-based statistical evaluation tasks. Lower panel: Overview of selected domains and packages covered in the benchmark. objectives, we prompt LLMs to generate natural user-style analytical queries. Each generated query is manually verified and paired with its corresponding ground-truth output to ensure correctness and reproducibility. Detailed task specifications and query generation prompts are provided in the Supplementary Materials. 4. Experiments 4.1. Experimental Setup We first generated synthetic user queries using MiMo-V2flash to simulate realistic user interactions. For each function, we generated 30 queries and designed five prompt templates ranging from simple to complex to reflect users with different levels of data analysis expertise. In total, this process produced 245,730 queries for the dataset. We split the dataset into training and test sets, using 85% of the data for training and the remaining 15% for testing. In our experiment, each query considers only one ground-truth item. The model is initialized with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 and trained using the AdamW (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019) optimizer for 100 epochs with batch size of 256 and an initial learning rate of 1 104. Training is conducted on an NVIDIA A100 (80GB) GPU. Evaluation is performed after each epoch, and the checkpoint achieving the best performance across multiple evaluation metrics is selected and released as DARE-R-Retriever (DARE). We evaluate the model using standard metrics commonly adopted in the information retrieval and search literature. Baselines. We compared DARE against wide range of open-sourced state-of-the-art embedding models, including BAAI/bge-m3 (Chen et al., 2024), intfloat/e5-large-v2 (Moreira et al., 2025), Snowflake/arctic-embed-l (Merrick, 2024), jinaai/jina-embeddings-v2-base-en (Gunther et al., 2024), mixedbread-ai/mxbai-embed-large-v1 (Lee et al., 2024), WhereIsAI/UAE-Large-V1 (Li & Li, 2024), AlibabaNLP/gte-large-en-v1.5 (Zhang et al., 2024), and sentencetransformers/all-mpnet-base-v2 (Song et al., 2020), as well as our base model sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019). To investigate the practical impact of integrating DARE into LLM agents for real-world statistical analysis workflows, we evaluate RCodingAgent across 16 statistical analysis tasks under two settings: without (w/o) and with DARE. Experiments are conducted across six representative LLMs, including deepseek-v3.2, gpt-5.2, mimo-v2-flash, grok-4.1fast, claude-haiku-4.5, and minimax-m2.1, to assess the robustness of DARE across diverse model capabilities. All agents are allowed at most 20 interaction steps for each task 6 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval attempt. 4.2. Evaluation Metrics Let denote the set of evaluation queries with size Q. For each query we obtain ranked list of retrieved functions (f(1), . . . , f(K)). Let rel(f ) {0, 1} indicate whether retrieved function is matched. Recall@k. For each query Q, we obtain ranked (cid:16) . We report list of retrieved functions Recall@k, defined as the fraction of queries whose groundtruth function appears in the top-k retrieved results: (1) , . . . , (q) (q) (K) (cid:17) Recall@k = 1 (cid:88) qQ I{ : rel(f (q) (j) ) = 1}. In particular, Recall@1 corresponds to top-1 accuracy. NDCG@k. To capture rank sensitivity, we also report NDCG@k. Define the discounted cumulative gain DCG@k = (cid:88) j=1 2rel(f(j)) 1 log2(j + 1) . Given set of evaluation queries Q, and the total inference time Tseq under sequential execution (batch size = 1): = 1000 (ms/query). Tseq Throughput (QPS): Measures the systems processing capacity under parallel execution to saturate the hardware. Given queries processed in optimal batches (e.g., batch size = 128) with total inference time Tbatch: QPS = Tbatch (queries/s). Success Rate (SR). To evaluate the end-to-end performance of LLM agents on the statistical analysis tasks, we define binary success indicator for each task. Let A(q) denote the execution output produced by the agent for given query q, and G(q) represent the corresponding ground-truth answer. The correctness of the agents result is determined by an indicator function: I(q) = (cid:40) 1, 0, if A(q) = G(q) otherwise . Let IDCG@k be the maximum achievable DCG@k under an ideal ranking; then The overall Success Rate is then defined as the proportion of tasks where the agents output matches the ground truth: NDCG@k = DCG@k IDCG@k . With binary relevance, IDCG@k = 1 whenever at least one relevant item exists, so NDCG@k reduces to logarithmically discounted score of how early the correct tool is retrieved. Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR@k). Evaluates how far down the ranked list the first relevant function appears. This metric is particularly critical for tool-augmented LLM agents, as placing the correct statistical tool higher in the prompt reduces token consumption and mitigates hallucination risks: MRR@k = 1 (cid:88) qQ 1 rank(q) , where rank(q) is the position of the first ground-truth function for query q. If no relevant function is retrieved within the top results, the reciprocal rank rank(q) is set to 0. Efficiency Metrics. To evaluate the deployment feasibility of DARE in real-time agentic workflows, we report two key efficiency indicators: Average Latency (L): Measures the mean wall-clock time required to encode single query-context pair. 7 SR = 1 (cid:88) qQ I(q). 5. Experimental Results In this section, we present comprehensive evaluation of DARE. First, we assess its retrieval performance against open-sourced embedding models on the RPKB test set. Second, we investigate the efficiency of DARE. Lastly, we examine whether integrating DARE into RCodingAgent improves performance across 16 downstream statistical analysis tasks. 5.1. Performance on Retrieval We compared DARE against diverse set of strong embedding baselines, ranging from compact sentence encoders (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2) to large-scale state-of-the-art models (e.g., BGE-M3 and Snowflake/arctic-embed-l). The results are summarized in Table 1. As shown in Table 1, DARE consistently establishes new state of the art across all reported retrieval metrics. In particular, DARE achieves an NDCG@10 of 93.47%, substantially outperforming the strongest baseline, Snowflake/arcticembed-l, by relative margin of 17.8%. This indicates that DARE not only retrieves the correct function but also ranks DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval Table 1. Performance comparison with open-sourced SoTA embedding models on RPKB test set. Despite having only 23M parameters, DARE significantly outperforms all other models (up to 568M parameters) across all retrieval metrics.

sections.content: 79.32% 75.13% 74.29% 73.08% 70.68% 70.66% 66.39% 66.06% 61.27% 93.47% 75.10% 70.86% 69.65% 68.43% 65.65% 65.56% 61.22% 60.57% 55.53% 91.76% 92.35% 88.38% 88.73% 87.58% 86.39% 86.58% 82.57% 83.30% 79.36% 98.63% 65.49% 61.52% 59.69% 58.47% 55.08% 54.79% 50.40% 49.37% 44.12% 87.39% it significantly higher than competing models. Moreover, DARE attains remarkably high Recall@1 of 87.39%, corresponding to 33.4% relative improvement over the best baseline, highlighting its strong ability to place the correct function at the top position. Beyond top-ranked accuracy, DARE also demonstrates superior overall retrieval coverage. It achieves Recall@10 of 98.63%, indicating that nearly all relevant functions are successfully retrieved within the top-10 candidates. In addition, the substantially higher MRR@10 of 91.76% reflects consistent ranking quality across queries, rather than gains driven by small subset of easy cases. Together, these results suggest that DARE delivers both strong early precision and robust ranking stability. key challenge in function retrieval lies in distinguishing between functions that are statistically similar yet distributionally distinct (e.g., differentiating glm from glm.nb). General-purpose embedding models, which are primarily optimized for semantic similarity, often struggle in such finegrained scenarios. In contrast, DARE explicitly conditions representation learning on distributional characteristics, enabling more precise discrimination among closely related statistical functions. This capability is directly reflected in the large gains observed in rank-sensitive metrics such as NDCG@10 and MRR@10. Notably, these performance improvements are achieved with remarkable efficiency. DARE adopts the architecture of all-MiniLM-L6-v2 and contains only 23M parameters, making it approximately 15 to 25 times smaller than leading competitors such as BAAI/bge-m3 (568M parameters) and Alibaba-NLP/gte-large (435M parameters). Despite its compact size, DARE substantially outperforms all large-scale baselines. Compared to the base model, DARE improves NDCG@10 from 61.27% to 93.47%, representing dramatic absolute gain of over 32 points. This result confirms that the proposed distribution-conditional contrastive learning framework effectively injects critical domain-specific Figure 5. Results of QPS and Latency. knowledge that is largely absent from the pre-training of general-purpose language models. 5.2. Inference Efficiency Analysis Beyond retrieval accuracy, inference latency and throughput are critical factors for deploying LLM agents in real-time, iterative data science workflows. Figure 5 illustrates the system performance trade-off between Latency (ms) and Throughput QPS across evaluated models. As visualized in Figure 5, DARE (represented by the green circle) dominates the efficiency landscape, occupying the ideal top-left corner. Our model achieves remarkable throughput of 8,512 QPS with an ultra-low latency of 3.7ms. This performance is attributed to the lightweight architecture of our base model and the simple and efficient design. In sharp contrast, high-performing general-purpose models such as BAAI/bge-m3, Snowflake/arctic-embed-l, and Alibaba-NLP/gte-large-en-v1.5 cluster in the bottom-right region. These models typically exhibit latencies exceeding 8 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval Table 2. Comparison of End-to-End Success Rates for Various LLM Agents on statistical analysis tasks, with and without the DARE Module.

sections.title: Model
sections.content: RCodingAgent (w/o DARE) RCodingAgent with DARE Claude-haiku-4.5 Deepseek-v3.2 Gpt-5.2 Grok-4.1-fast Mimo-v2-flash Minimax-m2.1 6.25% 18.75% 25.00% 18.75% 12.50% 12.50% 56.25% 56.25% 62.50% 75.00% 62.50% 68.75% 10ms and throughputs below 3,000 QPS due to their massive parameter counts (335M568M). Consequently, DARE offers speed advantage of approximately three to four times over these baselines. This efficiency is pivotal for agentic systems, where an agent may need to retrieve from thousands of candidate functions multiple times within single reasoning chain. DARE ensures that adding rigorous statistical retrieval introduces negligible overhead to the overall inference pipeline. 5.3. Impact on Agentic Data Analysis To validate the practical utility of DARE, we conducted experiments for RCodingAgent on 16 statistical analysis tasks. Our findings are largely consistent with prior studies (Orlanski et al., 2023; Zhao & Fard, 2025), revealing that current LLMs exhibit limited capability in effectively utilizing for statistical analysis tasks. As shown in Table 2, most models demonstrate very low end-to-end success rates on these tasks when relying solely on their inherent knowledge, highlighting substantial limitation of reliable statistical tool usage. Notably, Claude-haiku-4.5, Mimo-v2-flash, and Minimaxm2.1 initially showed poor performance on the benchmark. By contrast, augmenting these models with DARE leads to substantial performance improvements, increasing success rates to 50.00%, 50.00% and 56.25%, respectively. In addition, grok-4.1-fast exhibits the most pronounced improvement, with success rates rising from 18.75% to 75.00%, representing an absolute gain of 56.25%. Even for advanced frontier models, DARE consistently provides meaningful gains. For example, gpt-5.2 improves from 25.00% to 62.50%. These results indicate that DARE effectively bridges the statistical tool utilization gap, enabling both lightweight and frontier models to reliably perform specialized data science tasks. Figure 3 presents realistic example of statistical analysis. More cases can be found in the Supplementary Materials. By supplying precise, distribution-aware retrieval signals, DARE reduces execution failures and enhances the robustness of LLM agents in statistical analysis workflows. 6. Future Directions Despite the strong empirical performance of DARE, several promising research directions remain. Enhancing native proficiency of LLMs. Current LLMs exhibit limited native capability in statistical computing with R, partly due to the limited presence of high-quality R-oriented corpora in large-scale pretraining datasets. Future work may explore constructing large-scale R-centric knowledge corpora, such as curated tutorials, package documentation, and executable analytical workflows, to improve the internal statistical reasoning and programming proficiency of LLMs and complement retrieval-based augmentation. Advancing structured tool learning and utilization. Improving tool learning strategies remains an important direction. In our current framework, retrieved statistical packages are provided to the LLM through demonstration-style contextual augmentation using structured JSON descriptions. While effective, this approach may not fully capture the hierarchical and compositional relationships among statistical tools. Future work could investigate more structured and adaptive strategies, such as dynamic tool abstraction, function-level reasoning graphs, or memory-based tool selection mechanisms, to better support complex statistical tool usage. Expanding and sustaining the statistical knowledge base. Although our current repository contains over 8,191 highquality functions, the broader ecosystem includes substantially larger collection of domain-specific packages and specialized tools. We plan to open-source our curated knowledge base to encourage community-driven expansion, with the goal of establishing comprehensive and continuously evolving statistical knowledge infrastructure for LLM-based agents. Integrate RCodingAgent into mixture-of-experts agent system. promising direction is to integrate RCodingAgent into mixture-of-experts agent system. In this setting, RCodingAgent would serve as specialized expert for R-based statistical analysis, coordinated with other agents 9 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval responsible for complementary tasks. Such modular design could improve scalability and flexibility in complex end-to-end analytical workflows.

ACM Transactions on Software Engineering and Methodology, 34(8):139, 2025. 11 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval A. Example Prompt of Data Profile Generation This section presents an example prompt used to convert documentation into structured Data Profiles. It instructs an LLM to exclude generic utilities and extract key statistical metadata such as data modality, distribution assumptions, and dimensionality.

sections.title: Example Prompt of Data Profile Generation
sections.content: You are an expert Data Scientist and Developer. Your task is to filter and annotate functions for RetrievalAugmented Generation (DARE) system specialized in data science. You will receive JSON describing an function (including package description, function description, usage, arguments). Your goal is to: 1. FILTER: Determine if this function is specific algorithm, statistical method, data transformation, or machine learning model. - REJECT basic utilities (e.g., file I/O, simple string manipulation, getters/setters, pure plotting without analytics). - KEEP only if it has clear data input requirements and analytical purpose. 2. EXTRACT: If the function is relevant, extract structured Data Information. Specifically, what kind of data does this algorithm expect? (e.g., sparse matrices, categorical factors, normally distributed vectors). Output must be valid JSON object adhering to this schema: { is relevant: boolean, data profile: { data modality: string enum [tabular, time-series, text, image, graph, genomic/sequence, other], feature type: string enum [numerical, categorical, mixed, binary, text-token, any], distribution assumption: string (e.g., normal, sparse, poisson, any, unknown), dimensionality: string enum [low, high, any], missing data handling: string (e.g., handles na, must be complete, unknown), specific constraints: [list, of, strings] }, task type: string (e.g., classification, clustering, normalization, gene signature scoring), reasoning: short explanation } B. Example Prompt of DARE Training Query Generation Here is an example prompt to generate queries from selected functions. The generated queries are used to train the DARE model.

sections.title: Example Prompt of DARE Training Query Generation
sections.content: You are an expert Data Scientist creating training dataset for Semantic Search Engine. Your goal is to generate **30 diverse user search queries** that would lead user to the target function described below. 12 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval **CRITICAL RULES:** 1. **NEVER mention the function name or the package name** in the queries. The user doesnt know the tool exists yet; they are describing their problem. 2. **HEAVILY RELY on the Data Information**: The queries MUST describe the data structure, distribution, or constraints provided in the context (e.g., have sparse matrix..., My data is high-dimensional..., Handling zero-inflated count data...). 3. **Variety**: - 12 queries should be long and detailed (e.g., am working with large tabular dataset that has high sparsity, and need to...). - 5 queries should be problem-focused (e.g., How to handle missing values in categorical time-series?). - 5 queries should be Help me style (e.g., need way to plot gene expressions where the data is...). - 3 queries should be Instruction first then followed by data information (e.g. need to visualize the structure of protein... Data Information: type: tabular, distribution: sparse..... ) - 5 queries should be short/keyword-based but include data types (e.g., dim reduction for sparse arrays). **Input Format:** Function Description + Data Profile JSON. **Output Format:** valid JSON object with single key queries containing list of 30 strings. Example: queries: [query 1, query 2, ...] C. Example of RPKB Here are some examples of the RPKB, which contains the function description and the data profile.

sections.title: Example of RPKB
sections.content: { "id": 22, "fc_id": "Reproducible_Data_Screening_Checks_and_Report_of _Possible_Errors::makeDataReport", "ground_truth_doc": "Data Constraints: {"data_modality": "tabular", " feature_type": "mixed", "distribution_assumption": "any", "dimensionality ": "any", "missing_data_handling": "handles_na", "specific_constraints": ["data.frame/tibble /matrix", "handles labelled/haven_labelled as factors", "smartNum treats low unique nums as factors"]}. Task: data_quality_assessment. nR Package: Reproducible_Data_Screening_Checks_and_Report_of_Possible Errors. Package Description: Data screening is an important first step of any statistical thoroughn analysis. dataReporter auto generates customizable data report with summary of the checks ...... "data_profile": { "data_modality": "tabular", "feature_type": "mixed", "distribution_assumption": "any", "dimensionality": "any", "missing_data_handling": "handles_na", "specific_constraints": [ "data.frame/tibble/matrix", "handles labelled/haven_labelled as factors", "smartNum treats low unique nums as factors" ] }, "usage_guidance": "makeCodebook(data, vol = "", reportTitle = NULL, file = NULL, ...)", "example_code": "library(dataReporter) data(testData) 13 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval data(toyData) check(toyData) DF <- data.frame(x = 1:15) makeDataReport(DF) data(testData) makeDataReport(testData) # Overwrite any existing files generated by makeDataReport makeDataReport(testData, replace=TRUE) # Change output format to Word/docx: makeDataReport(testData, replace=TRUE, output = "word") # Only include problematic variables in the output document makeDataReport(testData, replace=TRUE, onlyProblematic=TRUE) # Add user defined check-function to the checks performed on character variables: # Here we add functionality to search for the string wally ( ignoring case) wheresWally <- function(v, ...) { res <- grepl("wally", v, ignore.case=TRUE) problem <- any(res) message <- "Wally was found in these data" checkResult(list(problem = problem, message = message, problemValues = v[res])) } wheresWally <- checkFunction(wheresWally, description = "Search for the string wally ignoring case", classes = c("character") ) # Add the newly defined function to the list of checks used for makeDataReport(testData, checks = setChecks(character = defaultCharacterChecks(add = " replace=TRUE) #Handle non-supported variable classes using treatXasY: treat characters. wheresWally")), raw as character and lists are not #treat complex as numeric. We also add list variable, but as #handled through treatXasY, this variable will be caught in the preChecks and skipped: toyData$rawVar <- as.raw(c(1:14, 1)) toyData$compVar <- c(1:14, 1) + 2i toyData$listVar <- as.list(c(1:14, 1)) makeDataReport(toyData, replace = TRUE, treatXasY = list(raw = "character", complex = "numeric"))", "task_type": "data_quality_assessment", "domain": "Specialized Statistics & ML" }

sections.content: { "id": 960, "fc_id": "OPTICS_K-Xi_Density-Based_Clustering::fortify_ica", "ground_truth_doc": "Data Constraints: {"data_modality": "tabular", " feature_type": dimensionality": "high", "missing_data_handling": "must_be_complete", " "numerical", "distribution_assumption": "non-gaussian", " 14 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval specific_constraints": ["numeric matrix", "suitable for blind source separation"]}. This package provides novel density-based cluster extraction method, OPTICS kXi, and framework to compare k-Xi models using ...... "feature_type": "numerical", "distribution_assumption": "non-gaussian", "dimensionality": "high", "missing_data_handling": "must_be_complete", "specific_constraints": [ "numeric matrix", "suitable for blind source separation" ] }, "usage_guidance": "fortify_ica(m_data, ..., sup_vars = NULL)", "example_code": "library(opticskxi) library(fastICA) library(plyr) df_ica <- fortify_ica(iris[-5], n.comp = 2) df_ica", "task_type": "independent_component_analysis", "domain": "Specialized Statistics & ML" }

sections.title: Example of RPKB
sections.content: { "id": 2460, "fc_id": "Estimate_a_Log-Concave_Probability_Density_from_I id_Observations::logconTwoSample", "ground_truth_doc": "Data Constraints: {"data_modality": "tabular", " feature_type": "numerical", "distribution_assumption": "log-concave", " dimensionality": "low", "missing_data_handling": "must_be_complete", " specific_constraints": ["univariate iid observations", "two samples for comparison"]}. Task: two-sample hypothesis test. "data_profile": { ...... "data_modality": "tabular", "feature_type": "numerical", "distribution_assumption": "log-concave", "dimensionality": "low", "missing_data_handling": "must_be_complete", "specific_constraints": [ "univariate iid observations", "two samples for comparison" ] }, "usage_guidance": "logconTwoSample(x, y, which = c("MLE", "smooth"), = 999, "example_code": "library(logcondens) n.grid = 500, display = TRUE, seed0 = 1977) ", n1 <- 30 n2 <- 25 <- rgamma(n1, 2, 1) <- rgamma(n2, 2, 1) + 1 twosample <- logconTwoSample(x, y, which = c("MLE", "smooth")[1], = 999) DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval ", "task_type": "two-sample hypothesis test", "domain": "Specialized Statistics & ML" } D. Training and Evaluation Details To further support the experimental analysis, we provide some details in training process. Figure 6. Training loss curves for the DARE model, demonstrating convergence behavior over training epochs. E. Example System Prompt For RCodingAgent Here is an example system prompt for the RCodingAgent.

sections.title: Example System Prompt For RCodingAgent
sections.content: You are an advanced Data Science Agent specializing in R. Your goal is to solve complex data analysis tasks by writing robust, efficient, and tidy code. Core Guidelines: 1. File I/O Safety: - Input: specific data files are located at {data path}. ONLY load data from this directory. - Output: save all results (CSVs, models, images) to {working path}. 2. Package Management: Install any packages if you need. 3. Task Execution: Solving the problem by multiple step, but finally you only need to give concise answer with numerical results. 4. System Integrity: IMPORTANTLY: You cannot perform any actions that may harm the system, such as deleting directories, important software, or software packages. 16 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval Figure 7. Evaluation loss curves for DARE in training process.

sections.title: Example System Prompt For RCodingAgent with DARE
sections.content: You are an advanced Data Science Agent specializing in equipped with package retrieval system. Your Ability: You will be provided with Retrieved Documentation. Use these documents to solve the task. - You MUST carefully analyze the Retrieved Documentation. - If the documents provide specific function, prioritize using it over general knowledge. - Mimic the usage style, syntax, and pay attention to example or usage sections. Core Guidelines: 1. File I/O Safety: Input from {data path}, output to {working path}. 2. Task Execution: Solve the problem by multiple steps, and give concise answer with numerical results. 3. System Integrity: Do not perform actions that harm the system. 17 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval F. Construction of Statistical Analysis Evaluation Tasks We use the following prompt to generate evaluation queries from real code and data.

sections.title: Construction of Statistical Analysis Evaluation Tasks
sections.content: Role: You are an expert in programming and advanced statistics assisting in research evaluation. Your task is to generate natural language queries based on function metadata to test the retrieval and execution capabilities of an AI Agent. Core Objective: The queries must never mention the package or function names. Instead, they must deeply embed the Distribution Assumptions, Data Modalities, and Specific Constraints found in the metadata. The goal is to force the model to retrieve the correct tool based on statistical logic rather than name memorization. Task Instructions (4 Query Styles): 1. Beginner Style (Vague): Focus on the high-level scientific goal without technical jargon. 2. Practitioner Style (Rough): Use standard industry terminology and describe the operational context. 3. Rigorous Researcher Style (Detailed): Explicitly include statistical profiles (e.g., Poisson, Gaussian, Time-series). This is the core test for DAREs performance. 4. Technical Logic Style (Parametric): Emphasize specific parameter configurations and the exact output required for evaluation. Hard Constraints for Output: - Mandatory Seed: Every query must require the user to start the script with set.seed(123). - Mandatory Verification: Every query must instruct the agent to print specific value (the Ground Truth) for evaluation (e.g., print the p-value or output the first value of est a). - Pre-loaded Data: Assume the dataset is already available. The Agent must read the data directly (e.g., from CSV or specific object name). - No Leakage: Strictly forbid mentioning the fc id or the Package name in the query. Example Format: have dataset [Data Modality] that follows [Distribution Assumption]. My goal is to [Task Type]. Please write an script starting with set.seed(123) to process the data... Finally, print [Ground Truth Metric] so can verify the results. G. Example of Statistical Analysis Task Here is an example task, illustrating the structure of the task type, data profile, ground truth verification, and the generated evaluation queries in four distinct styles. Example (ID: 960) Task Type: Independent Component Analysis Data Profile: Tabular, Numerical, Non-Gaussian Distribution, High-dimensional. Ground Truth Verification: df ica$IC1[1] = 0.5040647 df ica$IC2[1] = -1.372758 18 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval Generated Evaluation Queries: 1. Beginner Style: have file named iris data.csv containing multiple numeric measurements. suspect there are several independent underlying signals mixed together in this data, and want to separate them into 2 distinct components. Can you write an script that extracts these signals and adds them as new columns to the original data? Please set the seed to 123 for consistency and show me the first row of the two newly created columns. 2. Practitioner Style: need to perform feature extraction on iris data.csv to find independent components. Please use to reduce the numeric features to 2 components. Id like the results to be integrated back into the dataset (fortified). Make sure to set.seed(123) at the start and print the first row of the components so can verify the values. 3. Rigorous Researcher Style: am working with iris data.csv, which is high-dimensional numeric matrix. Given that the data follows non-Gaussian distribution, need to perform Independent Component Analysis (ICA) for blind source separation. Please extract 2 components and ensure they are fortified into the data frame. Set the random seed to 123 and output the first row of ICA1 and ICA2 to confirm the computation. 4. Technical Logic Style: Using the numeric data in iris data.csv, need to run density-based ICA extraction to get 2 components. Its crucial that the method supports fortify mechanism to merge the components back into the observation table automatically. Use seed 123 for reproducibility and display the top row of the resulting ICA columns. 19 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval H. Details of 16 Statistical Analysis Evaluation Tasks This section details the 16 representative statistical analysis tasks used to evaluate the RCodingAgent. Table 3. Detailed profile of representative evaluation tasks. Task Domain Detailed Information Independent component analysis Package/Function: OPTICS K-Xi Density-Based Clustering::fortify ica Two-sample hypothesis test Goodness-of-fit Age heaping detection Description: Density-based clustering utilities and visualization for ICA results Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/opticskxi/ vignettes/opticskxi.pdf Package/Function: Estimate Log-Concave Probability Density from Iid Observations::logconTwoSample Description: Two-sample hypothesis testing based on log-concave density estimation. Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/logcondens/logcondens.pdf Package/Function: National Information Platforms for Nutrition Anthropometric Data Toolkit::ageChildren Description: Anthropometric age processing and child age standardization utilities. Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/nipnTK/nipnTK.pdf Package/Function: National Information Platforms for Nutrition Anthropometric Data Toolkit::ageHeaping Description: Detection and correction of age heaping phenomena in demographic data Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/nipnTK/nipnTK.pdf Narrow endemic identification Package/Function: sity::getNarrowEndemics Auxiliary Functions to Estimate Centers of BiodiverDescription: Identification of narrow endemic species for biodiversity analysis. Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/sperich/sperich.pdf Environmental affinity assignment Package/Function: Diversity Dynamics using Fossil Sampling Data::affinity Description: Environmental affinity assignment for fossil occurrence records Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/divDyn/divDyn.pdf Survivorship analysis Package/Function: Diversity Dynamics using Fossil Sampling Data::survivors Conditional graphical model estimation Description: Survivorship analysis across temporal fossil sampling intervals Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/divDyn/divDyn.pdf Package/Function: Conditional Graphical LASSO (cglasso) Description: Estimation of conditional graphical models under censoring and missingness Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/cglasso/cglasso.pdf Clinical EHR preprocessing Package/Function: Tool::processLastDose Electronic Health Record Data Processing and Analysis Description: Temporal standardization and preprocessing of clinical dosage records. Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/EHR/EHR.pdf Clinical trial simulation Package/Function: Clinical Trial Simulation::cut data by event Regulatory score estimation Description: Survival data truncation based on event occurrence time. Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/simtrial/simtrial.pdf Package/Function: Estimating Regulatory Scores and Identifying ATAC-STARR Data::sharpr2 Description: Regulatory activity score estimation from high-throughput genomic assays Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/sharpr2/sharpr2.pdf 20 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval Task Domain Detailed Information Ancestry score computation Package/Function: Computation of Ancestry Scores with Mixed Families and Unrelated Individuals::perfectwhiten Linear mixed model Package/Function: Various Linear Mixed Model Analyses::lmm.jack Description: Covariance-preserving whitening for ancestry score computation. Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/PCFAM/PCFAM.pdf Gaussian mixture overlap Description: Linear mixed-effects modeling with jackknife-based inference Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/minque/minque.pdf Package/Function: Simulating Data to Study Performance of Clustering Algorithms::overlap Description: Computation of Gaussian mixture overlap for clustering evaluation. Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/MixSim/MixSim.pdf Factor Analysis Preprocessing Package/Function: Set of Assumptions for Factor and Principal Component Analysis::communalities Description: Preprocessing and communality estimation for factor analysis. Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/FactorAssumptions/FactorAssumptions.pdf Financial quality scoring Package/Function: Quality Scores for the Russell 3000::market profitability Description: Profitability z-scoring and financial quality metric computation. Link: https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/qmj/qmj.pdf I. Example Case: Performance Analysis of DARE on Data Analysis Task Here is an example for RCodingAgent with and without the DARE module for statistical analysis tasks.

sections.title: Query and Ground Truth
sections.content: User Query: have high-dimensional genomic dataset named hidra ex 1 2000.csv in my environment. need to identify driver elements by estimating regulatory scores based on the counts provided in the data. Please set the random seed to 123 at the start. need to filter for fragment lengths between 150 and 600 bp and use DNA count filter of 5. For my evaluation, please print the first value of the estimated scores (est a) for the very first region identified. Ground Truth: -0.2185 I.1. DARE By explicitly conditioning the query on distribution-aware metadata, DARE retrieves the correct statistical primitive. DARE Retrived Top 3 { 1. [ID: Estimating_Regulatory_Scores_and_Identifying_ATAC-STARR_Data::sharpr2] (Dist: 0.7704) Package: Estimating_Regulatory_Scores_and_Identifying_ATAC-STARR_Data :: sharpr2 {function_name: sharpr2, package_version: 1.1.1.0, package_name: Estimating_Regulatory_Scores_and_Identifying_ATAC-STARR_Data, task_type: regulatory_score_estimation, data_profile_json: {"data_modality": "genomic/sequence", "feature_type": "numerical", "distribution_assumption": " poisson", "dimensionality": "high", "missing_data_handling": "must_be_complete", "specific_constraints": ["ATAC-STARR dataset", "columns: start, end, PLASMID, RNA", "non-negative reals or integers", "one chromosome", "fragment lengths 150-600 bp", "RNA/DNA count filters"]}, function_details_json: {"name": "sharpr2", "title": "sharpr2", "description": "For HiDRA dataset on given chromosome, this function calls tiled regions (the regions covered by at least one fragment), and calculates regulatory scores for each tiled region. The regulatory scores are based on standardized log(RNA /PLASMID).", "usage": "sharpr2(data, l_min = 150, l_max = 600, f_rna = 10, f_dna = 0,n s_a = 300, verbose = FALSE, auto = TRUE, sig = TRUE, len = FALSE, alpha = 0.05, win = 5, mse = FALSE, max_t = 1)", "arguments": {"data": "A data. 21 DARE: Aligning LLM Agents with the Statistical Ecosystem via Distribution-Aware Retrieval frame containing an ATAC-STARR dataset for one chromosome. The data.frame must contain four columns: start, end, PLASMID, RNA. PLASMID and RNA are the values for DNA and RNA, which should be non-negative real numbers ( average value over multiple replicates) or integers (counts).", "l_min": "The fragments with length smaller than l_min will not be processed. The default is 150.", "l_max": "The fragments with length larger than l_max will not be processed. The default is 600.", "f_rna": "The fragments with an RNA count smaller than f_rna will not be processed. The default is 10.", "f_dna": "The fragments with an DNA count smaller than f_rna will not be processed. The default is 0.", "s_a": "A variance hyperparameter in the prior for the latent regulatory scores. The default is 1000.", "verbose": "An indicator of whether to show processing information. The default is FALSE.", "auto": "An indicator of whether to automatically estimate the ridge coefficient lambda from the data for each tiled region using data-driven way described in the reference. The default is TRUE. If auto is TRUE, s_a is ignored and ridge coefficient is estimated for each tiled region separately. If auto is FALSE, global user-defined ridge coefficient (1/s_a) is used.", "sig": "An indicator of whether to identify significant motif regions for the estimated scores. Only valid if auto=TRUE. The default is TRUE.", "len": "An indicator of whether to model log(RNA/PLASMID) of each fragment as the average or the sum of the latent regulatory scores. The default is FALSE, which is the sum.", "alpha": "A regional FWER to call high resolution driver elements (the significant regulatory region). The default is 0.05.", "win": "A window size for removing sporadic identified significant regions. If significant consecutive region is small than win , it will be treated as false signals.
